questions.explanation: Simple Past -> Past Perfect in backshift. But 'was reading' (past continuous) -> 'had been reading'. Select 'had been' — wait: was reading -> had been reading. None of above perfectly. 'had been' not an option. 'was reading' can stay in indirect if no backshift needed. Use 'was'.

questions.question: How is a direct question converted to indirect?
options: Using 'if/whether' for yes-no questions or WH-word for WH-questions, with statement word order
questions.explanation: Indirect questions: if/whether (yes/no) or WH-word + subject + verb (no inversion). No question mark.

questions.question: Direct: He asked, 'Are you coming?' Indirect: He asked _______ I was coming.
questions.explanation: Yes/No question -> whether/if. He asked whether I was coming.

questions.question:
Assertion: 'He said, I am well' is incorrect punctuation.
Reason: The comma should appear before opening quotation marks and the direct speech must be enclosed in quotes: 'He said, I am well.'
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Direct speech needs quotation marks and comma. R correctly explains the punctuation rule.

questions.question: Direct: 'Where do you live?' he asked. Indirect: He asked _______ I lived.
questions.explanation: WH-question: keep WH-word + subject + verb (statement order). Where I lived.

questions.question: Which time expression change is WRONG in indirect speech?
options: yesterday -> the previous day / the day before
questions.explanation: Tomorrow -> the next day / the following day. 'Next day' without 'the' is less precise.

questions.question: Direct: He said, 'Go away!' Indirect: He _______ to go away.
questions.explanation: Commands: told/ordered/commanded + object + to + base verb. Told me to go away.

questions.question:
Assertion: Universal truths do not change tense in indirect speech.
Reason: Facts that are always true (the sun rises in the east) stay in present tense even in reported speech.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: 'The teacher said the earth revolves around the sun.' Present tense retained. R correctly identifies.

questions.question: Direct: 'Please help me,' she said. Indirect: She _______ to help her.
questions.explanation: Please (polite request) -> requested/pleaded + object + to + verb. Requested him to help her.

questions.question: Direct: 'Can you swim?' she asked. Indirect: She asked _______ I could swim.
questions.explanation: Yes/no question -> whether. She asked whether I could swim.

questions.question: Which pronoun change is correct in indirect speech?
options: He said, 'I am fine.' -> He said that he was fine.
She said, 'You are wrong.' -> She said that I was wrong. (if I am listener)
He said, 'We will go.' -> He said that they would go.
questions.explanation: All three are correct pronoun adjustments. D is the right answer.

questions.question: Direct: 'I had seen that film,' she said. Indirect: She said that she _______ that film.
questions.explanation: Past Perfect in direct -> stays Past Perfect in indirect (already most past). Had seen.

questions.question:
Assertion: 'This' changes to 'that' and 'these' changes to 'those' in indirect speech.
Reason: Demonstratives shift from proximity (direct speech context) to distance (reported speech context).
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: This/these (near speaker then) -> that/those (distant now in report). R correctly explains.

questions.question: Direct: 'May I come in?' the student asked. Indirect: The student asked _______ he might come in.
questions.explanation: Permission question: whether + subject + might. Whether he might come in.

questions.question: Which time word does NOT change in indirect speech?
questions.explanation: Always, never, sometimes, often = timeless adverbs that don't need to change.

questions.question: Direct: 'We shall win,' they said. Indirect: They said that they _______ win.
questions.explanation: Shall -> would in indirect speech.

questions.question:
Assertion: Exclamatory sentences in direct speech become statements with appropriate reporting verb in indirect speech.
Reason: Exclamations are reported using verbs like exclaimed, shouted, cried out, with 'that'.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: 'What a lovely day!' -> She exclaimed that it was a lovely day. R correctly identifies reporting verbs.

questions.question: Direct: 'Don't make noise,' said the teacher. Indirect: The teacher _______ not to make noise.
questions.explanation: Negative command: told/ordered + object + not to + base verb. Told us not to make noise.

questions.question: Which 'say/tell' usage is correct?
options: He said me that he was ill
She told that she would come
He said that he was tired
questions.explanation: Say + (that) + clause. Tell + object + that + clause. Say does not take an object before 'that'.

questions.question: Direct: 'How beautiful the painting is!' Indirect: He exclaimed _______ the painting was very beautiful.
questions.explanation: Exclamatory -> exclaimed + that + subject + verb. Exclaimed that the painting was very beautiful.

questions.question:
Assertion: If the reporting verb is in present tense, no tense backshift is needed.
Reason: Backshift occurs when reporting verb is past; present tense reporting keeps original tenses.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: 'She says she is fine' (present reporting, no backshift). 'She said she was fine' (past, backshift). R correctly identifies.

questions.question: Direct: He said to me, 'Are you happy?' Indirect: He asked me _______ I was happy.
questions.explanation: Yes/no question -> whether. Whether I was happy.

questions.question: 'He said that he would finish the work the _______ day.' (replacing 'next' in direct speech)
questions.explanation: Tomorrow/next day -> the following day in indirect speech.

questions.question: Which sentence uses a modal verb?
questions.explanation: Can is a modal verb. Modals: can, could, may, might, shall, should, will, would, must, ought to.

questions.question: The modal verb 'must' expresses:
questions.explanation: Must = strong obligation (You must wear a seatbelt) or logical certainty (He must be tired).

questions.question: 'You should see a doctor' expresses:
questions.explanation: Should = advice, recommendation, or mild obligation.

questions.question: Which is NOT a modal verb?
questions.explanation: Run is a regular action verb, not a modal. Modals: can, could, may, might, shall, should, will, would, must.

questions.question: 'She may come to the party' expresses:
questions.explanation: May = possibility (She may come - perhaps she will) or polite permission (May I come in?).

questions.question: 'Could you help me?' compared to 'Can you help me?' is:
questions.explanation: Could makes a request more polite and tentative than can. Used for formal/polite requests.

questions.question:
Assertion: Modal verbs do not change form for different subjects.
Reason: We say 'he can', not 'he cans' - modals have no -s, -ed, or -ing forms.
options: Both A and R true, R explains A
Both A and R true, R does not explain A
questions.explanation: Modals are invariable: no conjugation for person, number, or tense. R correctly explains.

questions.question: 'He would visit every Sunday when he lived nearby' uses 'would' to express:
questions.explanation: Would for past habit describes a repeated action in the past (same as used to).

questions.question: 'You ought to apologise' is similar in meaning to:
questions.explanation: Ought to = should = moral obligation or advice. Softer than must.

questions.question: Which sentence is grammatically CORRECT?
questions.explanation: Should is correct - no conjugation needed. Cans, musts, wills are all wrong forms.

questions.question: 'It might rain this afternoon' expresses:
questions.explanation: Might = weak or tentative possibility. Less certain than may.

questions.question: 'She must be the new manager' uses must to express:
questions.explanation: Must for deduction: based on evidence I conclude this must be true.

questions.question: 'Will you close the door?' is a:
questions.explanation: Will in question form = polite request. Also functions as future simple question.

questions.question: 'She _______ speak French when she was five' - which modal fits? (past ability)
questions.explanation: Could = past ability. She could speak French when she was five.

questions.question: Which modal expresses prohibition (not allowed)?
questions.explanation: Must not = prohibition. You must not smoke here = you are not allowed to smoke.

questions.question: 'I will finish this by Friday' expresses:
questions.explanation: Will = future intention, promise, or prediction.

questions.question: Which modal is NOT used for future prediction?
questions.explanation: Must is NOT typically used for future prediction. Will, shall, may, might, could are used for prediction.

questions.question: 'Shall I carry your bag?' uses shall to:
options: Make an offer or seek instruction
questions.explanation: Shall in questions with I/we = offer or seeking instructions/suggestions.

questions.question:
Assertion: 'Must' and 'have to' both express obligation but differ in source.
Reason: 'Must' = internal/personal obligation; 'have to' = external rule or requirement imposed by others.
options: Both A and R true, R explains A
Both A and R true, R does not explain A
questions.explanation: I must exercise (self-imposed) vs I have to wear a uniform (rule imposed by school). R correctly distinguishes.

questions.question: 'You needn't come if you are busy' means:
options: You do not have to come
questions.explanation: Needn't = need not = there is no obligation/necessity to come.

questions.question: '_______ you please pass the salt?' - which modal makes this most polite?
questions.explanation: Could you please = most polite form of this request. More formal than can or will.

questions.question: 'She cannot be at work - I just saw her at the market' uses 'cannot' to express:
questions.explanation: Cannot for logical impossibility: it is impossible that she is at work given the evidence.

questions.question: Which modal-verb combination is INCORRECT in standard English?
questions.explanation: Can is already a modal - saying 'can to go' is wrong. Modals are followed by bare infinitive (without to), except ought to.

questions.question: 'Would you like some tea?' is:
questions.explanation: Would you like = a polite way to make an offer or invitation.

questions.question: The modal 'shall' is now most commonly used in:
options: Formal British English, offers, and legal documents
questions.explanation: Shall is formal and more common in British English, legal language, and first-person offers/suggestions.

questions.question:
Assertion: Modal verbs are always followed by the base form of the verb.
Reason: Modals take bare infinitive (verb without to), e.g. 'can go', 'must leave', 'should try'.
options: Both A and R true, R explains A
Both A and R true, R does not explain A
questions.explanation: Base/bare infinitive after modals is a core grammar rule. R correctly states and exemplifies this.

questions.question: 'You _______ not park here after 6pm' - which modal expresses a prohibition?
questions.explanation: Must not = prohibition. You must not park here = it is forbidden to park here.

questions.question: 'If I were rich, I would travel the world' uses 'would' in:
questions.explanation: Would in second conditional = hypothetical or unlikely future scenario.

questions.question: Which sentence uses a modal INCORRECTLY?
questions.explanation: Mights is not a word - modals do not take -s. Correct: He might arrive late.

questions.question: 'May I borrow your pen?' uses 'may' to ask for:
questions.explanation: May I = polite request for permission.

questions.question: A clause is a group of words that contains:
questions.explanation: A clause must have both a subject and a verb at minimum.

questions.question: An INDEPENDENT (main) clause can:
options: Only be part of a sentence
Stand alone as a complete sentence
questions.explanation: Independent clause = complete thought; can stand as a sentence on its own.

questions.question: A DEPENDENT (subordinate) clause:
options: Can stand alone as a sentence
Cannot stand alone; depends on a main clause
questions.explanation: Dependent clause: has subject + verb but is not a complete thought without a main clause.

questions.question: Which is NOT an independent clause?
options: The sun sets in the west.
questions.explanation: Although he was tired is a dependent clause - it cannot stand alone (begins with subordinating conjunction).

questions.question: 'When the alarm rang, she woke up.' What is 'When the alarm rang'?
questions.explanation: When the alarm rang = adverb clause, answering when she woke up. It is dependent.

questions.question: 'The book that I borrowed was fascinating.' The clause 'that I borrowed' is an:
questions.explanation: That I borrowed = relative/adjective clause modifying the noun book.

questions.question: 'I know what she said.' The clause 'what she said' functions as:
questions.explanation: What she said = noun clause, functioning as the object of the verb know.

questions.question:
Assertion: A sentence can contain more than one clause.
Reason: Compound and complex sentences combine two or more clauses using conjunctions.
options: Both A and R true, R explains A
Both A and R true, R does not explain A
questions.explanation: Multiple clauses joined by conjunctions = compound/complex sentences. R correctly explains.

questions.question: Which sentence is NOT a complex sentence?
options: Although it rained, we went out.
She left because she was tired.
questions.explanation: He sang and she danced = compound sentence (two independent clauses joined by 'and'). The others are complex.

questions.question: A COMPOUND sentence contains:
options: One independent and one dependent clause
Two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction
questions.explanation: Compound: two independent clauses joined by for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so (FANBOYS).

questions.question: Subordinating conjunctions introduce:
questions.explanation: Subordinating conjunctions (although, because, when, if, since) introduce dependent clauses.

questions.question: Which is NOT a subordinating conjunction?
questions.explanation: But is a coordinating conjunction. Although, because, and since are subordinating.

questions.question: An ADVERB clause modifies:
options: A verb, adjective, or another adverb - answers when, where, why, how
questions.explanation: Adverb clause: modifies verb/adjective/adverb, answering when, where, why, how, etc.

questions.question: An ADJECTIVE (relative) clause modifies:
questions.explanation: Adjective/relative clause modifies a noun/pronoun, introduced by who, which, that, whom, whose.

questions.question: In 'Whoever arrives first wins the prize', the underlined clause 'Whoever arrives first' is a:
questions.explanation: Whoever arrives first = noun clause, functioning as the subject of the sentence.

questions.question: A sentence with one independent clause and one or more dependent clauses is called a _______ sentence.
questions.explanation: One independent + one (or more) dependent clause = complex sentence.

questions.question: 'He finished his work, and she made dinner.' This is a:
questions.explanation: Two independent clauses joined by and = compound sentence.

questions.question:
Assertion: A noun clause can function as subject, object, or complement in a sentence.
Reason: Like a noun, a noun clause can fill any position in a sentence that a noun can fill.
options: Both A and R true, R explains A
Both A and R true, R does not explain A
questions.explanation: Noun clauses are versatile - can be subject, object, complement. R correctly explains using noun analogy.

questions.question: 'The man who called you is waiting outside.' The relative clause is:
options: Who called you (adjective clause modifying 'the man')
The man who called you is waiting
questions.explanation: Who called you = relative clause modifying the man. This is the adjective clause.

questions.question: A CONDITIONAL clause typically begins with:
questions.explanation: Conditional clauses begin with if, unless, provided (that), as long as, on condition that.

questions.question: 'Because she studied hard, she passed the exam.' 'Because she studied hard' is a(n) _______ clause.
questions.explanation: Because = subordinating conjunction introducing an adverb clause of reason.

questions.question: A CONCESSIVE clause expresses contrast or unexpected result, introduced by:
questions.explanation: Concessive clauses = although, even though, while, whereas - introduce contrasting ideas.

questions.question: Which sentence contains a NOUN clause?
options: I believe that she is honest.
questions.explanation: I believe THAT SHE IS HONEST - the bold clause functions as the object of believe = noun clause.

questions.question: A compound-complex sentence contains:
options: At least two independent clauses AND at least one dependent clause
questions.explanation: Compound-complex = 2+ independent + 1+ dependent clauses.

questions.question: 'The cake that she baked was delicious.' Identify the type of clause underlined.
questions.explanation: That she baked = relative/adjective clause modifying the noun cake.

questions.question:
Assertion: Relative clauses can be defining or non-defining.
Reason: Defining clauses identify which person or thing is meant; non-defining add extra information (set off by commas).
options: Both A and R true, R explains A
Both A and R true, R does not explain A
questions.explanation: Defining: The man who called you (specifies which man). Non-defining: My father, who is a doctor, ... R correctly distinguishes.

questions.question: 'If it rains, we will cancel the event.' The clause 'If it rains' is a(n) _______ clause.
questions.explanation: If it rains = conditional adverb clause answering under what condition.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of a dependent clause?
options: Can stand alone as a complete sentence
Depends on the main clause for meaning
questions.explanation: Dependent clauses CANNOT stand alone - that is why they are called dependent.

questions.question: 'Tell me where you live.' The clause 'where you live' functions as:
questions.explanation: Where you live = noun clause acting as the direct object of tell.

questions.question: A SIMPLE sentence contains:
options: One independent clause (and no dependent clauses)
Only a subject with no verb
questions.explanation: Simple sentence = one independent clause expressing a complete thought.

questions.question: Spot the error: 'She don't like coffee.'
options: don't should be doesn't (3rd person singular)
questions.explanation: 3rd person singular needs doesn't. She doesn't like coffee.

questions.question: Spot the error: 'He has went to Delhi.'
options: went should be gone (Present Perfect needs V3)
questions.explanation: Has + V3. Went = V2. Should be: He has gone to Delhi.

questions.question: Error: 'She is more taller than her sister.' The error is in _______.
questions.explanation: More taller = double comparative. Correct: taller than.

questions.question: Spot the error: 'Neither of the two girls are present.'
questions.explanation: Neither takes singular verb. Neither of the two girls is present.

questions.question: Error: 'He is one of the best student in the class.' Correct: 'He is one of the best _______ in the class.'
questions.explanation: One of the + plural noun. One of the best students.

questions.question: Spot the error: 'The news are shocking.'
options: are should be is (news is singular)
questions.explanation: News = uncountable singular. The news is shocking.

questions.question: Error: 'I have been living here _______ five years.' Choose correct preposition.
questions.explanation: Duration (five years) needs 'for'. I have been living here for five years.

questions.question: Spot the error: 'He is knowing the answer.'
options: knowing should be know (stative verb - no continuous)
questions.explanation: Stative verb. He knows the answer (not is knowing).

questions.question: Error: 'She told that she was busy.' Correct: 'She _______ that she was busy.'
questions.explanation: Tell + object (tell me). Say + that (no object). She SAID that she was busy. (not 'told that')

questions.question: Spot the error: 'He is elder than his brother.'
options: elder should be older (elder used only in family, not comparisons with 'than')
questions.explanation: Elder: no 'than'. Older than (used with than). He is older than his brother.

questions.question: Error: 'She writes with a good handwriting.' Correct: 'She has _______ handwriting.'
questions.explanation: Handwriting is uncountable. She has good handwriting (no article).

questions.question: Spot the error: 'He said me that he was ill.'
options: said should be told (tell + object; say has no object before 'that')
questions.explanation: Said me = error. Say does not take an indirect object. He TOLD me that he was ill.

questions.question: Error: 'I did not understood the lesson.' Correct: 'I did not _______ the lesson.'
questions.explanation: Did + not + base verb. I did not understand.

questions.question: Spot the error: 'She is more beautiful than any girl in her class.'
options: any girl should be any other girl (she is part of the class)
questions.explanation: Comparing within a group: any OTHER girl. Otherwise she compares with herself too.

questions.question: Error: 'He was playing cricket _______ it started raining.' Choose the correct conjunction.
questions.explanation: Two simultaneous past events: when. He was playing when it started raining.

questions.question: Spot the error: 'The scenery of Kashmir are very beautiful.'
questions.explanation: Scenery = uncountable. The scenery is beautiful.

questions.question: Error: 'Each of the students have submitted their homework.' Correct: 'Each of the students _______ submitted their homework.'
questions.explanation: Each = singular. Has submitted.

questions.question: Spot the error: 'Hardly had she reached home when it began to rain.'
options: No error — this is correct inverted structure
questions.explanation: Hardly had... when = correct. Inversion after negative adverb. No error.

questions.question: Error: 'He always come late to class.' Correct: 'He always _______ late to class.'
questions.explanation: Always = habitual. 3rd person singular: comes.

questions.question: Spot the error: 'He is a M.A. in English.'
options: a should be an (M.A. begins with vowel sound 'em')
questions.explanation: M.A. pronounced 'em ay' = vowel sound. An M.A. (not a M.A.).

questions.question: Error: 'The police has arrested the thief.' Correct: 'The police _______ arrested the thief.'
questions.explanation: Police = always plural noun. The police have arrested.

questions.question: Spot the error: 'She prefers tea more than coffee.'
options: more than should be just 'to' (prefer X to Y = correct idiom)
questions.explanation: Prefer X to Y (not more than). She prefers tea to coffee.

questions.question: Error: 'He has a little friends.' Correct: 'He has _______ friends.'
questions.explanation: Friends = countable. Few/a few for countable. A few friends (a few = some).

questions.question: Spot the error: 'I look forward to see you soon.'
options: see should be seeing (look forward to + V-ing)
questions.explanation: To (preposition) + V-ing. Look forward to SEEING you.

questions.question: Error: 'She was working hardly.' Correct: 'She was working _______.'
questions.explanation: Hard = adverb (working hard). Hardly = barely/scarcely. She was working HARD.

questions.question: Spot the error: 'He is married with a beautiful woman.'
questions.explanation: Married to (not with). He is married to a beautiful woman.

questions.question: Error: 'My all friends are coming.' Correct: '_______ my friends are coming.'
questions.explanation: All of my / All my (not 'my all'). All of my friends are coming.

questions.question: Spot the error: 'She gave me many informations about the topic.'
options: Both A and B are errors
questions.explanation: Information = uncountable (no plural). Much information. Both 'many' and 'informations' are wrong.

questions.question: Error: 'They discussed about the problem.' Correct: 'They _______ the problem.'
questions.explanation: Discuss is transitive and does not take 'about'. They discussed the problem.

questions.question: Spot the error: 'We reached to the station on time.'
options: reached to should be reached (reach is transitive, no 'to')
on time should be in time
questions.explanation: Reach does not need 'to'. We reached the station. (not 'reached to').

questions.question: Choose the correct sentence:
questions.explanation: He doesn't play cricket = correct (3rd person singular, doesn't + base verb).

questions.question: Correct sentence for present perfect:
questions.explanation: I have completed = correct present perfect (I + have + V3).

questions.question: Choose the correct passive sentence:
options: The letter was wrote by her
The letter was written by her
The letter were written by her
The letter is wrote by her
questions.explanation: Was written = correct simple past passive (was + V3).

questions.question: Choose the correct form: 'He is the _______ boy in the class.' (tall)
questions.explanation: Superlative with 'the': the tallest. (not most tall — one-syllable adjectives use -est)

questions.question: Select the sentence without error:
options: He is more taller than his friend
She is the most cleverest girl
This is the most difficult problem
He is more clever than she
questions.explanation: The most difficult = correct. Others have double comparatives/superlatives.

questions.question: Correct the sentence: 'She has been working here _______ 2019.'
questions.explanation: Since + point in time. She has been working here since 2019.

questions.question: Choose the sentence with correct subject-verb agreement:
options: My father and mother is coming
The team are winning the match (British)
Either the students or the teacher are responsible
Neither he nor his friends was present
questions.explanation: The team are winning (British English collective noun — acceptable). Others have agreement errors.

questions.question: Correct: 'She _______ here since she was a child.' (live)
questions.explanation: Since + duration up to present = Present Perfect Continuous. Has been living.

questions.question: Choose the correctly punctuated direct speech:
questions.explanation: Direct speech: comma before open quote, capital inside, close quote at end.

questions.question: Correct the sentence: 'She went to the market _______ (buying) vegetables.'
questions.explanation: Purpose: to + base verb. She went to the market to buy vegetables.

questions.question: Which sentence correctly uses comparative?
options: She is more better than him
She is more good than him
questions.explanation: Better = irregular comparative of good. No 'more'. She is better than him.

questions.question: Correct: 'Neither the teacher nor the students _______ present.' (is/are)
options: are (proximity rule: students = plural, nearest to verb)
questions.explanation: Proximity rule: nearest subject (students = plural) determines verb. Are.

questions.question: Choose the correctly structured reported speech:
options: She said that she is happy
He said that he was going to Delhi
He asked that are you coming
questions.explanation: He said that he was going to Delhi = correct indirect speech (tense backshift).

questions.question: Correct: 'I look forward _______ you at the conference.'
questions.explanation: Look forward to + V-ing (to = preposition). To seeing.

questions.question: Choose the sentence without article error:
questions.explanation: An honest (vowel sound /o/). He is an honest worker.

questions.question: Correct: 'You should _______ more water every day.'
questions.explanation: Should + base verb. Should drink.

questions.question: Which sentence uses 'since' correctly?
options: I have been here since three hours
She has been ill since Monday
They worked here since 2020 (needs have been)
questions.explanation: Since Monday = point in time + Present Perfect. She has been ill since Monday.

questions.question: Choose correct preposition: 'She is fond _______ music.'
questions.explanation: Fond of = fixed expression. Fond of music.

questions.question: Select the correct sentence (active/passive):
options: The cake ate by the children
The cake was ate by the children
The cake was eaten by the children
The children were eaten the cake
questions.explanation: Was eaten by the children = correct simple past passive (was + V3).

questions.question: Correct: 'He plays _______ guitar very well.' (article)
questions.explanation: Musical instruments: the guitar. He plays the guitar very well.

questions.question: Choose the sentence with correct modal usage:
options: He can to swim very fast
She must to submit the form today
I would to like some tea
questions.explanation: Should complete = correct. Others incorrectly add 'to' after modals.

questions.question: Correct: 'By this time next year, she _______ her degree.' (complete - future perfect)
questions.explanation: Future Perfect: will have + V3. Will have completed.

questions.question: Choose the sentence with correct tense sequence:
options: When he arrived, she has left
When he arrived, she had already left
When he arrives, she had left
questions.explanation: Past Perfect (had left) for earlier action before Simple Past (arrived). Correct.

questions.question: Correct: 'I _______ my homework before my mother arrived.' (complete)
questions.explanation: Completed before another past action = Past Perfect. Had completed.

questions.question: Select the correctly formed question:
options: Did she went to school yesterday?
Had she go to school yesterday?
Did she go to school yesterday?
Does she went to school yesterday?
questions.explanation: Did + base form. Did she go? (not went)

questions.question: Correct: 'The scissors _______ on the table.' (is/are)
questions.explanation: Scissors = always plural. Are on the table.

questions.question: Which is a correctly formed negative sentence?
questions.explanation: Did + not + base verb. She did not go home.

questions.question: Correct: 'The committee _______ reached a decision.' (American English)
questions.explanation: American English: committee = singular. Has reached.

questions.question: Correct use of 'few' and 'a few':
options: She has few friends (and this implies she wishes she had more)
questions.explanation: Few friends (countable, negative implication: not many). Few = countable, little = uncountable.

questions.question: Correct: 'He is _______ than any other student in his class.' (intelligent - superlative in comparison)
questions.explanation: Comparing one with all others: more intelligent than any other (not superlative form in this structure).

questions.question: SYNONYM means:
questions.explanation: Synonyms: words with same or similar meaning. Happy = joyful.

questions.question: Synonym of HAPPY:
questions.explanation: Happy = joyful, glad, content, cheerful.

questions.question: Synonym of BRAVE:
questions.explanation: Brave = courageous, fearless, bold, valiant.

questions.question: Synonym of BEAUTIFUL:
questions.explanation: Beautiful = charming, attractive, lovely, gorgeous.

questions.question: Which is NOT a synonym of ANGRY?
questions.explanation: Calm = opposite of angry. Furious/irate/enraged = synonyms of angry.

questions.question: Synonym of DIFFICULT:
questions.explanation: Difficult = arduous, challenging, hard, tough.

questions.question: Synonym of ABUNDANT:
questions.explanation: Abundant = plentiful, ample, copious, profuse.

questions.question: Which is NOT a synonym of HUMBLE?
questions.explanation: Arrogant = opposite of humble. Modest/meek/unassuming = synonyms.

questions.question: Synonym of FAST:
questions.explanation: Fast = quick, rapid, swift, speedy.

questions.question: Synonym of CEASE:
questions.explanation: Cease = stop, halt, discontinue, terminate.

questions.question: Synonym of RICH:
questions.explanation: Rich = wealthy, affluent, prosperous, opulent.

questions.question: Which is a synonym of SAD?
questions.explanation: Melancholy = sad, sorrowful. Others = antonyms.

questions.question: Synonym of ANCIENT:
questions.explanation: Ancient = old, archaic, antique, age-old.

questions.question: Synonym of TRANSPARENT:
questions.explanation: Transparent = clear, see-through, translucent.

questions.question: Which is NOT a synonym of GENEROUS?
questions.explanation: Miserly = mean/stingy = opposite of generous.

questions.question: Synonym of DILIGENT:
questions.explanation: Diligent = hardworking, industrious, assiduous, painstaking.

questions.question: Synonym of BEGIN:
questions.explanation: Begin = commence, start, initiate, launch.

questions.question: Synonym of CONSPICUOUS:
questions.explanation: Conspicuous = prominent, noticeable, obvious, salient.

questions.question: Synonym of TACITURN:
questions.explanation: Taciturn = reserved, uncommunicative, reticent, silent.

questions.question: Synonym of DESTROY:
questions.explanation: Destroy = demolish, ruin, devastate, annihilate.

questions.question: Which is NOT a synonym of OBSTINATE?
questions.explanation: Flexible = opposite of obstinate. Stubborn/tenacious/headstrong = synonyms.

questions.question: Synonym of ELOQUENT:
questions.explanation: Eloquent = fluent, articulate, expressive, silver-tongued.

questions.question: Synonym of IMMENSE:
questions.explanation: Immense = enormous, vast, colossal, gigantic.

questions.question: Synonym of FRUGAL:
questions.explanation: Frugal = thrifty, economical, sparing. Extravagant/wasteful/lavish = opposite.

questions.question: Synonym of AMBIGUOUS:
questions.explanation: Ambiguous = vague, unclear, equivocal, doubtful.

questions.question: Synonym of BENEVOLENT:
questions.explanation: Benevolent = kind, charitable, generous, philanthropic.

questions.question: Synonym of PRUDENT:
questions.explanation: Prudent = wise, sensible, judicious, cautious.

questions.question: Synonym of FEROCIOUS:
questions.explanation: Ferocious = fierce, savage, brutal, vicious.

questions.question: Synonym of AFRAID:
questions.explanation: Afraid = terrified, scared, frightened, fearful.

questions.question: Synonym of VERBOSE:
questions.explanation: Verbose = wordy, long-winded, loquacious, garrulous.

questions.question: ANTONYM means:
questions.explanation: Antonyms: opposite meaning. Hot <-> Cold.

questions.question: Antonym of HAPPY:
questions.explanation: Happy <-> Sad, Miserable, Unhappy.

questions.question: Antonym of BRAVE:
questions.explanation: Brave <-> Timid, Cowardly, Fearful.

questions.question: Antonym of ANCIENT:
questions.explanation: Ancient <-> Modern, New, Contemporary. All three are antonyms.

questions.question: Antonym of DIFFICULT:
questions.explanation: Difficult <-> Easy, Simple, Effortless.

questions.question: Antonym of RICH:
questions.explanation: Rich <-> Poor, Impoverished, Penniless.

questions.question: Antonym of TRANSPARENT:
questions.explanation: Transparent <-> Opaque (cannot see through). Clear/obvious/visible = synonyms.

questions.question: Antonym of EXPAND:
questions.explanation: Expand <-> Contract, Shrink, Reduce, Compress.

questions.question: Antonym of DAY:
questions.explanation: Day <-> Night.

questions.question: Antonym of GENEROUS:
questions.explanation: Generous <-> Miserly, Stingy, Mean. Others are synonyms of generous.

questions.question: Antonym of VIRTUOUS:
questions.explanation: Virtuous <-> Wicked, Sinful, Immoral.

questions.question: Antonym of VICTORY:
questions.explanation: Victory <-> Defeat, Loss, Failure.

questions.question: Antonym of LOVE:
questions.explanation: Love <-> Hatred, Hate, Dislike.

questions.question: Antonym of OPTIMISTIC:
questions.explanation: Optimistic <-> Pessimistic, Cynical, Despairing.

questions.question: Antonym of SLOW:
questions.explanation: Slow <-> Fast, Quick, Rapid, Swift.

questions.question: Antonym of DILIGENT:
questions.explanation: Diligent <-> Lazy, Idle, Indolent.

questions.question: Antonym of ARTIFICIAL:
questions.explanation: Artificial <-> Natural, Real, Genuine, Authentic.

questions.question: Antonym of ASCEND:
questions.explanation: Ascend <-> Descend, Fall, Drop.

questions.question: Antonym of TRIVIAL:
questions.explanation: Trivial <-> Important, Significant, Major, Crucial.

questions.question: Antonym of ACCEPT:
questions.explanation: Accept <-> Reject, Refuse, Decline.

questions.question: Antonym of SINCERE:
questions.explanation: Sincere <-> Insincere, Dishonest, Hypocritical.

questions.question: Antonym of BENEVOLENT:
questions.explanation: Benevolent <-> Malevolent, Cruel, Hostile.

questions.question: Antonym of PERMANENT:
questions.explanation: Permanent <-> Temporary, Transient, Fleeting.

questions.question: Antonym of ARROGANT:
questions.explanation: Arrogant <-> Humble, Modest, Meek.

questions.question: Antonym of COMMENCE:
questions.explanation: Commence <-> End, Conclude, Finish, Terminate.

questions.question: Antonym of FLEXIBLE:
questions.explanation: Flexible <-> Rigid, Stiff, Inflexible.

questions.question: Antonym of TRUTH:
questions.explanation: Truth <-> Falsehood, Lie, Untruth.

questions.question: Antonym of LOQUACIOUS:
questions.explanation: Loquacious <-> Taciturn, Reserved, Silent.

questions.question: Antonym of OBSCURE:
questions.explanation: Obscure <-> Famous, Clear, Prominent, Obvious.

questions.question: Antonym of FRUGAL:
questions.explanation: Frugal <-> Extravagant, Wasteful, Lavish.

questions.question: One who cannot die:
questions.explanation: Immortal = one who cannot die (im- = not, mortal = subject to death).

questions.question: One who can speak two languages: _______
questions.explanation: Bilingual = speaks two languages (bi = two).

questions.question: A place where books are kept and issued: _______
questions.explanation: Library = place for books and reading.

questions.question: One who eats only plants:
questions.explanation: Herbivore = eats only plants. Carnivore = meat. Omnivore = both.

questions.question: One who studies stars and planets: _______
questions.explanation: Astronomer = scientist who studies stars, planets, galaxies.

questions.question: A person who travels to a holy place: _______
questions.explanation: Pilgrim = one who journeys to a holy/sacred place.

questions.question: One who is present everywhere:
questions.explanation: Omnipresent = present everywhere. Omnivore = eats everything (food sense).

questions.question: Something that cannot be corrected: _______
questions.explanation: Incorrigible = impossible to correct or reform.

questions.question: A study of the origin and history of words: _______
questions.explanation: Etymology = study of word origins and history.

questions.question: A doctor who specialises in children's diseases:
questions.explanation: Paediatrician = children's doctor. Cardiologist = heart. Gynaecologist = women's health.

questions.question: One who believes in God: _______
questions.explanation: Theist = believes in God/gods. Atheist = no belief in God.

questions.question: A person who lives alone and avoids company: _______
questions.explanation: Hermit = recluse, one who lives in isolation.

questions.question: One who writes for a newspaper: _______
questions.explanation: Journalist = writes for newspapers/media. Author = book writer.

questions.question: Government by the people: _______
questions.explanation: Democracy = government by the people (demos = people, kratos = power).

questions.question: A disease that spreads quickly and widely: _______
questions.explanation: Epidemic = widespread disease in a region. Pandemic = worldwide.

questions.question: One who cannot read or write:
questions.explanation: Illiterate = unable to read or write.

questions.question: A list of books available in a library: _______
questions.explanation: Catalogue = list of items in a library or collection.

questions.question: Murder of a king: _______
questions.explanation: Regicide = killing of a king (regi = king, -cide = killing).

questions.question: One who hates mankind:
questions.explanation: Misanthrope = hates people. Philanthropist/altruist = loves/helps people.

questions.question: Something that is very old and no longer in use: _______
questions.explanation: Obsolete = out of use, no longer current.

questions.question: A speech made without preparation: _______
questions.explanation: Extempore = impromptu speech, without preparation.

questions.question: One who is all-knowing:
questions.explanation: Omniscient = all-knowing (omni = all, scient = knowing).

questions.question: Fear of heights: _______
questions.explanation: Acrophobia = fear of heights (acro = heights).

questions.question: A person who donates money for good causes: _______
questions.explanation: Philanthropist = lover of humanity; donates to charitable causes.

questions.question: A government run by one person with absolute power:
questions.explanation: Autocracy = rule by one person with absolute power (auto = self, cracy = rule).

questions.question: The study of the mind: _______
questions.explanation: Psychology = study of the mind and behaviour.

questions.question: A place where animals are kept: _______
questions.explanation: Zoo = place where animals are kept and displayed.

questions.question: One who has many skills and interests:
questions.explanation: Polymath/Renaissance person = versatile person with many skills/knowledge areas.

questions.question: Writing that cannot be read easily: _______
questions.explanation: Illegible = cannot be read. Legible = readable.

questions.question: The science of human development and culture: _______
questions.explanation: Anthropology = study of human development, culture, and societies.

questions.question: An IDIOM is:
options: A phrase whose meaning cannot be guessed from individual words
A figure of speech comparing two things
questions.explanation: Idioms: 'kick the bucket' doesn't literally mean kicking a bucket; means to die.

questions.question: 'Beat around the bush' means:
options: To hit bushes with a stick
questions.explanation: Beat around the bush = avoid the main point, not speaking directly.

questions.question: 'It's raining cats and dogs' means:
questions.explanation: Raining cats and dogs = raining very heavily.

questions.question: 'Break a leg' means:
questions.explanation: Break a leg = good luck (used in theatre/performances).

questions.question: 'Bite the bullet' means:
options: To endure a difficult situation with courage
questions.explanation: Bite the bullet = endure pain/hardship bravely.

questions.question: 'Under the weather' means:
questions.explanation: Under the weather = feeling sick or unwell.

questions.question: 'Hit the nail on the head' means:
questions.explanation: Hit the nail on the head = say exactly what is correct.

questions.question: 'A penny for your thoughts' means:
options: Asking someone what they are thinking about
questions.explanation: A penny for your thoughts = I'd like to know what you're thinking.

questions.question: 'Burning the midnight oil' means:
questions.explanation: Burning the midnight oil = working/studying very late at night.

questions.question: 'Let the cat out of the bag' means:
questions.explanation: Let the cat out of the bag = accidentally reveal a secret.

questions.question: 'Add fuel to the fire' means:
questions.explanation: Add fuel to the fire = make an already bad situation even worse.

questions.question: 'Once in a blue moon' means:
questions.explanation: Once in a blue moon = very rarely, almost never.

questions.question: 'Spill the beans' means:
questions.explanation: Spill the beans = reveal secret information unintentionally.

questions.question: 'Bite off more than you can chew' means:
options: Take on more than you can handle
questions.explanation: Bite off more than you can chew = take on more responsibility than you can manage.

questions.question: 'On the fence' means:
questions.explanation: On the fence = undecided, not yet taking a position.

questions.question: 'Pull someone's leg' means:
questions.explanation: Pull someone's leg = tease or joke with someone.

questions.question: 'Cost an arm and a leg' means:
questions.explanation: Cost an arm and a leg = extremely expensive.

questions.question: 'Hit the sack' means:
questions.explanation: Hit the sack = go to bed; time to sleep.

questions.question: 'Piece of cake' means:
questions.explanation: Piece of cake = something very easy to do.

questions.question: 'Give the cold shoulder' means:
questions.explanation: Give the cold shoulder = deliberately ignore someone; show indifference.

questions.question: 'Sit on the fence' means:
options: To literally sit on a fence
To avoid taking sides in a disagreement
questions.explanation: Sit on the fence = avoid committing to one side; remain neutral.

questions.question: 'Barking up the wrong tree' means:
options: Pursuing the wrong course/blaming the wrong person
questions.explanation: Barking up the wrong tree = wrong assumption, pursuing wrong solution.

questions.question: 'Cry over spilt milk' means:
options: Complain about past events that cannot be changed
questions.explanation: Cry over spilt milk = waste time grieving over something that can't be undone.

questions.question: 'The ball is in your court' means:
options: It is your turn/responsibility to act
questions.explanation: The ball is in your court = it is now your responsibility to take the next step.

questions.question: 'Miss the boat' means:
questions.explanation: Miss the boat = miss an opportunity; be too late to take advantage.

questions.question: 'Get out of hand' means:
questions.explanation: Get out of hand = become impossible to control; get out of control.

questions.question: 'In the nick of time' means:
options: Just in time; at the last moment
questions.explanation: In the nick of time = just in time, at the last possible moment.

questions.question: 'Bury the hatchet' means:
options: End a dispute and make peace
questions.explanation: Bury the hatchet = end a quarrel; make peace; reconcile.

questions.question: 'Wear your heart on your sleeve' means:
questions.explanation: Wear your heart on your sleeve = openly show your feelings/emotions.

questions.question: 'Get a taste of your own medicine' means:
options: Receive the same treatment you give to others
questions.explanation: Get a taste of your own medicine = experience the same negative things you do to others.

questions.question: AFFECT (verb) vs EFFECT (noun): 'The rain _______ the match.'
questions.explanation: Affect (verb) = to influence. The rain affects the match. Effect (noun) = the result.

questions.question: 'The treatment had a positive _______ on his health.' (effect/affect)
questions.explanation: Effect (noun) = result. A positive effect.

questions.question: 'Accept' vs 'Except': 'Please _______ our invitation.'
questions.explanation: Accept = receive/agree to. Except = excluding. Please accept our invitation.

questions.question: 'Everyone _______ Ram attended the party.' (accept/except)
questions.explanation: Except = excluding. Everyone except Ram.

questions.question: 'Their', 'There', 'They're': 'The students forgot _______ books.'
questions.explanation: Their = possessive. Their books.

questions.question: '_______ is my bag.' (there/their/they're)
questions.explanation: There = that place. There is my bag. (pointing to location)

questions.question: 'Principal' vs 'Principle': The _______ of the school addressed the students.
questions.explanation: Principal (noun/adj) = head of school / main. Principle (noun) = fundamental rule.

questions.question: He always acts on _______ of honesty. (principal/principle)
questions.explanation: Principle = a rule or belief. He acts on the principle of honesty.

questions.question: 'Lose' vs 'Loose': Don't _______ your keys. (lose/loose)
questions.explanation: Lose (verb) = misplace. Loose (adj) = not tight. Don't lose your keys.

questions.question: 'Your' vs 'You're': '_______ welcome here.'
questions.explanation: You're = you are. You're welcome. Your = possessive (your book).

questions.question: 'Complement' vs 'Compliment': The scarf _______ her outfit beautifully. (matches/goes well)
questions.explanation: Complement (verb) = go well with, complete. The scarf complements her outfit.

questions.question: He paid her a nice _______. (praise) (complement/compliment)
questions.explanation: Compliment (noun) = praise. He paid her a nice compliment.

questions.question: 'Stationary' vs 'Stationery': She bought pens and paper from a _______ shop.
questions.explanation: Stationery (noun) = writing materials. Stationary (adj) = not moving.

questions.question: The car remained _______ in traffic. (not moving)
questions.explanation: Stationary (adj) = not moving. The car was stationary.

questions.question: 'Then' vs 'Than': She is taller _______ her brother.
questions.explanation: Than = used in comparisons. Taller than.

questions.question: 'Then' usage: 'First we ate, _______ we slept.'
questions.explanation: Then = at that time / next in sequence. Then we slept.

questions.question: 'Advice' vs 'Advise': She gave me good _______ about the exam. (noun)
questions.explanation: Advice (noun) = guidance. She gave good advice. Advise (verb) = to recommend.

questions.question: I _______ you to study regularly. (advise/advice)
questions.explanation: Advise (verb) = to recommend. I advised you.

questions.question: 'Beside' vs 'Besides': She sat _______ me during the lecture.
questions.explanation: Beside (prep) = next to. Sat beside me. Besides (prep) = in addition to.

questions.question: 'Besides' in a sentence: _______ English, she also speaks French.
questions.explanation: Besides = in addition to. Besides English, she speaks French.

questions.question: 'Emigrate' vs 'Immigrate': She _______ from India to Canada. (left India)
questions.explanation: Emigrate = leave one's own country. Immigrate = enter a new country.

questions.question: He _______ to Australia from the UK. (entered Australia)
questions.explanation: Immigrate = enter and settle in a new country. He immigrated to Australia.

questions.question: 'Farther' vs 'Further': I cannot walk any _______ (physical distance).
questions.explanation: Farther = physical distance. Further = additional/metaphorical. I cannot walk any farther.

questions.question: We need to discuss this _______. (additional/metaphorical distance - further/farther)
questions.explanation: Further = additional, metaphorical. We need to discuss this further.

questions.question: 'Imply' vs 'Infer': The speaker _______ that the project would be delayed.
questions.explanation: Imply = suggest without stating directly (speaker implies). Infer = conclude from evidence (listener infers).

questions.question: From his silence, I _______ that he disagreed. (infer/imply)
questions.explanation: Infer = draw a conclusion. I inferred from his silence.

questions.question: 'Alternate' vs 'Alternative': She went to school on _______ days. (every other day)
questions.explanation: Alternate (adj) = every other. She went on alternate days.

questions.question: 'Is there an _______ route to the airport?' (another possible route)
questions.explanation: Alternative (adj) = another option. An alternative route.

questions.question: 'Continuous' vs 'Continual': The phone rang _______ all evening. (repeatedly, with breaks)
questions.explanation: Continual = repeated with interruptions. Continuous = without any break.

questions.question: The river flows _______ into the sea. (without any break/stop)
questions.explanation: Continuous = without stopping. The river flows continuously.

questions.question: Para-jumbles involve:
options: Arranging jumbled sentences into a coherent paragraph
questions.explanation: Para-jumbles: rearrange sentences in logical order to form a meaningful passage.

questions.question: In para-jumbles, the FIRST sentence typically:
options: Introduces the topic without referring to anything outside the passage
questions.explanation: Opening sentence: introduces the main idea. No external references or pronouns pointing backwards.

questions.question: Which type of sentence is likely the LAST in a para-jumble?
options: One that introduces a new topic
One that begins with 'However, this led to...'
One that gives a conclusion or summarises
questions.explanation: Last sentence: concludes, summarises, or gives the outcome.

questions.question:
Assertion: Pronouns (he, she, it, they, this, that) help identify the sequence in para-jumbles.
Reason: A pronoun must refer back to a previously mentioned noun; thus it comes AFTER the sentence with that noun.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Pronoun reference: noun first, pronoun later. R correctly explains the sequencing logic.

questions.question: Sentences: A. This made her extremely popular. B. She worked tirelessly for the poor. C. Mother Teresa dedicated her life to service. D. Soon the whole world knew her name. Correct order:
questions.explanation: C (introduces topic) -> B (explains what she did) -> A (result: popular) -> D (consequence: world fame).

questions.question: Which word signals a CONTRAST and helps identify sentence sequence?
questions.explanation: However/nevertheless = contrast with previous sentence. Comes after a contrasting statement.

questions.question: Clue words showing SEQUENCE or ADDITION include:
options: First, then, moreover, furthermore, in addition
questions.explanation: First/then/moreover/furthermore = sequential/additive connectors.

questions.question:
Assertion: 'It', 'he', 'she', 'they' cannot begin a paragraph.
Reason: These pronouns require an antecedent (the noun they refer to) which must appear first.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Pronouns without antecedent = vague. R correctly explains why pronoun sentences cannot start.

questions.question: Sentences: 1.He became India's most famous leader. 2.Gandhi was born in Porbandar in 1869. 3.He led the independence movement. 4.His non-violence philosophy inspired the world. Order:
questions.explanation: 2 (birth/intro) -> 3 (main achievement) -> 1 (result) -> 4 (legacy). Chronological+logical.

questions.question: Which linking word indicates a CAUSE-EFFECT relationship?
questions.explanation: Therefore/consequently = cause-effect. A, so B.

questions.question: Sentence ending with a colon (:) is likely followed by:
questions.explanation: Colon introduces what follows: a list, definition, or explanation.

questions.question:
Assertion: Definite article 'the' may indicate a sentence is NOT first in a sequence.
Reason: 'The' refers to something already known/mentioned; 'a/an' introduces something for the first time.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: The = known already. A/an = first mention. R correctly identifies the sequencing clue.

questions.question: Sentences: A. However, this led to his defeat. B. He was a great warrior. C. He won many battles. D. But overconfidence proved fatal. Order:
questions.explanation: B (introduce) -> C (battles won) -> A (contrast: defeat) -> D (reason: overconfidence). BCAD.

questions.question: 'In conclusion', 'To sum up', 'Finally' suggest:
questions.explanation: These phrases signal the concluding/final sentence of a paragraph.

questions.question: 'This view' or 'This idea' at the beginning of a sentence suggests it comes _______ the sentence expressing that view/idea.
questions.explanation: 'This view' refers back to a view just stated. Must follow the sentence with that view.

questions.question:
Assertion: Numbers, statistics, or specific data are usually not found in the opening sentence.
Reason: Data needs context; the opening sentence first establishes the topic before data is introduced.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Context before data. R correctly explains why data comes after topic introduction.

questions.question: In para-jumbles, 'consequently' links to:
options: A contrasting idea that comes before
A cause that comes before, producing this result
questions.explanation: Consequently = as a result. Cause comes before, consequence after.

questions.question: Which strategy is NOT helpful for para-jumbles?
questions.explanation: Counting letters has no logical relevance. All other strategies help.

questions.question: Sentences: 1.The results were disappointing. 2.The team practised hard for months. 3.Nevertheless, they did not give up. 4.Finally they won the championship. Order:
questions.explanation: 2 (practice) -> 1 (disappointing result) -> 3 (nevertheless: didn't give up) -> 4 (final success).

questions.question:
Assertion: Transitional phrases like 'on the other hand' signal that two contrasting points are being compared.
Reason: This phrase introduces the second of two contrasting views/ideas.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: 'On the other hand' = second contrasting point. Must follow first point. R correctly explains.

questions.question: 'For example', 'For instance', 'Such as' signal that the following sentence is a/an:
questions.explanation: These phrases introduce examples/illustrations of the previous statement.

questions.question: Which is a clue that a sentence is SECOND in sequence?
options: It begins with 'Furthermore' or 'Also' adding to a previous point
It ends with a question to the reader
questions.explanation: Furthermore/also = adding to previous sentence. Comes second or later.

questions.question: Sentences: P. It is one of the wonders of the world. Q. The Taj Mahal is a beautiful monument. R. It was built by Emperor Shah Jahan. S. It attracts millions of tourists every year. Order:
questions.explanation: Q (introduce Taj) -> R (built by) -> P (wonder of world) -> S (tourists). QRPS.

questions.question:
Assertion: The sentence containing the thesis or main idea is usually first in a paragraph.
Reason: A topic sentence introduces the main theme; all other sentences support or elaborate on it.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Topic sentence = first. Supporting sentences follow. R correctly explains paragraph structure.

questions.question: 'This is because...' at the beginning of a sentence means it explains the sentence that _______ it.
questions.explanation: This is because = reason for what was just stated. The explanation follows the statement.

questions.question: Identify the OPENING sentence: which one needs NO reference from earlier?
options: Sachin Tendulkar is regarded as one of the greatest cricketers
questions.explanation: Sachin Tendulkar... = introduces subject. No prior reference needed. Clear opening.

questions.question: When two sentences are joined by 'so', the cause comes _______ and the effect comes _______.
questions.explanation: Cause comes first, then 'so', then effect. It rained so we stayed inside.

questions.question:
Assertion: In para-jumbles, reading all sentences before sequencing is more efficient than ordering one by one.
Reason: Understanding the overall topic helps identify the logical sequence more accurately.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Read all, grasp topic, then sequence. More efficient strategy. R correctly explains.

questions.question: 'Despite this', 'Even so', 'All the same' indicate sentences that _______ a previous point.
questions.explanation: Despite this/even so = contrast/qualification. Concedes then continues.

questions.question: 'Later', 'Afterwards', 'Subsequently' signal:
questions.explanation: Later/afterwards/subsequently = time sequence, following a previous event.

questions.question: A CLOZE TEST involves:
options: Filling in blanks in a passage with appropriate words
questions.explanation: Cloze test: a passage with blanks; choose the most appropriate word for each blank.

questions.question: In a cloze test, the best word fits the blank based on:
questions.explanation: Correct cloze answer must fit grammatically AND make semantic sense in context.

questions.question: Which approach works best for cloze tests?
options: Read the entire passage first to understand context, then fill blanks
questions.explanation: Read whole passage for context and tone, then fill each blank.

questions.question:
Assertion: In cloze tests, reading the sentences before and after the blank helps determine the correct answer.
Reason: Context from surrounding sentences clarifies the meaning and grammar needed.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Context is key. R correctly identifies why surrounding sentences matter.

questions.question: Passage: 'Ravi was _______ (1) for the exam. He studied _______ (2) all night.' Fill (1):
questions.explanation: Nervous = adjective, fits 'was + adjective' structure.

questions.question: Fill (2) from the passage above: 'He studied _______ all night.'
questions.explanation: Studied + adverb. Diligently = adverb form.

questions.question: Passage: 'The old man walked _______ down the street.' Which word fits?
questions.explanation: Walked + adverb. Slowly = correct adverb form.

questions.question: Passage: 'She is known for her _______.' (quality of being kind) Choose:
questions.explanation: Kindness = noun form. Known for her kindness.

questions.question:
Assertion: Transition words like 'however', 'therefore', 'moreover' are commonly tested in cloze passages.
Reason: These words signal logical relationships and the correct one depends on whether ideas contrast, add, or conclude.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Transition words tested frequently. Must understand relationship (contrast vs addition). R correctly explains.

questions.question: Passage: 'He worked hard. _______, he did not pass the exam.' Best connector:
questions.explanation: Worked hard BUT didn't pass = contrast. However.

questions.question: Passage: 'The weather was sunny, _______ everyone was in a good mood.' Connector:
questions.explanation: Sunny therefore good mood = cause-effect. So.

questions.question: Passage: 'She is not only intelligent _______ also hardworking.' Connector:
questions.explanation: Not only... but also = paired connector. But also.

questions.question:
Assertion: In cloze tests, eliminating clearly wrong options helps narrow down choices.
Reason: Options that don't fit grammatically or contextually can be eliminated, leaving the best fit.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Process of elimination is a valid strategy. R correctly explains the two criteria.

questions.question: Passage: 'The project was completed _______ schedule.' (before the due time) Preposition:
questions.explanation: Ahead of schedule = before the deadline. Completed ahead of schedule.

questions.question: Passage: 'He is _______ the most talented player in the team.' Intensifier:
questions.explanation: Undoubtedly = without doubt, intensifies the claim. Fits the meaning.

questions.question: Passage: 'She _______ to accept the award gracefully.' Verb that fits 'accepted with grace':
questions.explanation: Managed to = succeeded in. She managed to accept gracefully.

questions.question:
Assertion: The tone of a passage (formal/informal) guides word choice in cloze tests.
Reason: Formal passages need formal vocabulary; informal passages allow casual language.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Match register: formal = utilise, commence. Informal = use, start. R correctly identifies tone importance.

questions.question: Passage: '_______ the rain, the match continued.' Preposition/conjunction showing concession:
questions.explanation: Despite = in spite of (takes noun/gerund). Despite the rain.

questions.question: Passage: 'The economy has been growing _______ for the past decade.' Adverb:
questions.explanation: Growing + adverb. Steadily = correct adverb form.

questions.question: Passage: 'He had _______ idea that the surprise party was planned.' Determiner:
questions.explanation: No idea = idiom meaning he was completely unaware.

questions.question:
Assertion: Collocations (natural word combinations) are important in cloze tests.
Reason: Certain words naturally go together (strong tea, heavy rain, make a decision); wrong collocations sound unnatural.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Collocations: strong tea (not powerful tea). R correctly explains why natural pairs matter.

questions.question: Passage: 'She _______ a decision to leave the company.' Verb collocation:
questions.explanation: Make a decision = standard collocation. Made a decision.

questions.question: Passage: 'It was a _______ day with clear skies.' Adjective meaning 'bright and warm':
questions.explanation: Clear skies = sunny day. Context clues confirm.

questions.question: Passage: 'The student worked _______ hard that she topped the exam.' Intensifier:
questions.explanation: So...that = result clause structure. Worked so hard that...

questions.question:
Assertion: Verb tense consistency in cloze tests helps determine the correct tense for blanks.
Reason: The dominant tense in the passage guides the tense of blank answers.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Tense must be consistent with the passage's time frame. R correctly identifies this strategy.

questions.question: Passage (past context): 'He _______ the book and began to read.' Verb:
questions.explanation: Past narrative: simple past. Picked up.

questions.question: Passage: 'She spoke _______ at the meeting.' Which fits 'in a hesitant manner'?
questions.explanation: Hesitantly = in a hesitant manner. Matches the description.

questions.question: Passage: 'The doctor advised him to _______ his diet.' Verb meaning to improve:
questions.explanation: Advised to change/improve diet = positive health advice.

questions.question:
Assertion: In cloze tests, the blank answer must complete the meaning of the full sentence AND connect logically to the paragraph.
Reason: Both local (sentence-level) and global (passage-level) context must be considered.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Two-level context: sentence + paragraph. R correctly identifies both levels of analysis.

questions.question: Passage: 'Education is the _______ to success.' Noun fitting 'key':
questions.explanation: Key to success = most natural idiom/collocation.

questions.question: Sentence completion tests your ability to:
options: Choose a word that logically and grammatically completes a sentence
questions.explanation: Sentence completion: select the most appropriate word or phrase to complete the sentence.

questions.question: Despite working hard, she _______ to pass the exam.
questions.explanation: Despite = concession. 'Despite working hard' but failed = logical contrast.

questions.question: The teacher asked the students to _______ from using mobile phones during class.
questions.explanation: Refrain from = avoid doing. Refrain from using phones.

questions.question: He was so exhausted that he could hardly _______.
questions.explanation: So exhausted = very tired. Hardly keep his eyes open = natural idiom for extreme tiredness.

questions.question: The government announced several _______ to encourage investment.
questions.explanation: Encourage investment = incentives (positive inducements).

questions.question:
Assertion: In sentence completion, both vocabulary and logic are tested.
Reason: The correct word must fit the grammatical structure AND make logical sense in context.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Grammar + logic both needed. R correctly identifies both requirements.

questions.question: She was _______ for her honesty and dedication to work.
questions.explanation: Honesty and dedication = positive qualities. Admired = praised for positive traits.

questions.question: The sun _______ in the west every evening.
questions.explanation: The sun sets in the west. (rises in east)

questions.question: The old building was _______ to make way for a modern complex.
questions.explanation: Demolished = torn down to make space. Opposite of constructed.

questions.question: She _______ the opportunity to study abroad.
questions.explanation: Seized the opportunity = took the chance eagerly.

questions.question:
Assertion: Context clues (surrounding words) help complete sentences.
Reason: Words like 'although', 'because', 'therefore' signal contrast, reason, or result and guide word choice.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Context clues crucial. Connectors signal relationship. R correctly explains.

questions.question: Although she was tired, she _______ to complete the assignment.
questions.explanation: Although (contrast) tired, she managed (succeeded) = logical.

questions.question: He _______ the award for best student of the year.
questions.explanation: Received the award = accepted it (positive outcome).

questions.question: The medicine had a _______ effect on his recovery.
questions.explanation: Context of medicine helping = positive effect.

questions.question: He spoke so _______ that everyone in the hall could hear him clearly.
questions.explanation: Everyone heard clearly = spoke loudly.

questions.question:
Assertion: Words with similar meanings can sometimes be distinguished by their collocations.
Reason: 'Make' collocates with 'decision', 'mistake'; 'do' collocates with 'homework', 'work' — choosing wrong one is an error.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Collocations distinguish near-synonyms. R correctly gives examples.

questions.question: The scientist _______ a new theory that revolutionised physics.
questions.explanation: Proposed a theory = put forward for consideration.

questions.question: She _______ the bus and had to wait for the next one.
questions.explanation: Missed the bus = arrived too late to board.

questions.question: The drought _______ thousands of farmers this year.
questions.explanation: Drought (negative event) affected farmers (caused harm/hardship).

questions.question: The _______ of the company has grown threefold in the last decade.
questions.explanation: Grown threefold = increased = revenue (income).

questions.question:
Assertion: Negative words (hardly, barely, never, seldom) require careful attention in sentence completion.
Reason: They change the meaning of the sentence and affect which option logically fits.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Negative words flip expected meaning. R correctly warns of this pitfall.

questions.question: He _______ a lot of time on social media instead of studying.
questions.explanation: Spends time on social media = uses time. Negative implication (instead of studying).

questions.question: The children were _______ with joy when they heard the news.
questions.explanation: Bursting/overwhelmed/filled with joy — all work, though 'overwhelmed' or 'filled' is most common.

questions.question: Despite the team's best efforts, the project _______ a failure.
questions.explanation: Turned out/proved to be/became = all work in this context.

questions.question: It is important to _______ a balance between work and personal life.
questions.explanation: Maintain a balance = keep equilibrium. Standard collocation.

questions.question:
Assertion: 'She insisted that he _______ (leave).' — the blank should be filled with 'leave' (subjunctive), not 'left'.
Reason: Verbs like insist, suggest, demand are followed by that-clause with subjunctive (base form).
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Subjunctive after insist/suggest: She insisted that he leave. R correctly identifies the grammar.

questions.question: The new policy aims to _______ corruption at all levels.
questions.explanation: Eradicate = completely eliminate. Policy aim = eradicate corruption.

questions.question: She _______ her best to complete the work on time.
questions.explanation: Did her best = tried as hard as possible. Standard collocation.

questions.question: After years of practice, the pianist _______ the art of performing.
questions.explanation: Years of practice -> mastered = became highly skilled.

questions.question: The new bridge will _______ the travel time between the two cities.
questions.explanation: A bridge connecting two places = reduce travel time.

questions.question: She _______ a book every week. (read - habit)
questions.explanation: Habit = Simple Present. 3rd person singular: reads.

questions.question: I _______ never met him before. (have/had)
questions.explanation: Never before meeting = Present Perfect. Have never met.

questions.question: The match _______ tomorrow at 5 PM. (future scheduled event)
questions.explanation: Timetable/scheduled: Simple Present. The match starts at 5 PM.

questions.question: She is _______ than her sister. (tall - comparative)
questions.explanation: Comparative of one-syllable adjective: -er. Taller than.

questions.question: This is the _______ film I have ever seen. (interesting - superlative)
questions.explanation: Two+ syllable adjective superlative: most interesting.

questions.question: Neither of the options _______ correct. (is/are)
questions.explanation: Neither = singular. Is correct.

questions.question: She told me _______ she would come. (that/whether)
questions.explanation: Statement reporting: said/told + that. Told me that she would come.

questions.question: He asked me _______ I had eaten. (if/that)
questions.explanation: Yes/no indirect question: if/whether. Asked if I had eaten.

questions.question: The report was written _______ the manager. (passive - by)
questions.explanation: Passive voice: by + agent. Written by the manager.

questions.question: She _______ to the market yesterday. (go)
questions.explanation: Yesterday = past. Simple past: went.

questions.question: I have been studying English _______ three years. (duration)
questions.explanation: Duration (three years) = for. For three years.

questions.question: The teacher, _______ I respect greatly, retired last year. (relative pronoun for person)
questions.explanation: Whom/who for people. Who I respect greatly (non-defining clause).

questions.question: Can you _______ me a favour? (do/make)
questions.explanation: Do a favour = standard collocation. Do me a favour.

questions.question: She is the only student _______ passed the exam. (who/which)
questions.explanation: Only student who passed = relative pronoun for person (subject). Who.

questions.question: It is raining heavily. You should carry _______ umbrella.
questions.explanation: Umbrella starts with vowel sound /u/. An umbrella.

questions.question: She _______ here since she was a child. (live - Present Perfect Continuous)
questions.explanation: Since + duration to present = Present Perfect Continuous. Has been living.

questions.question: He is fond _______ music. (preposition)
questions.explanation: Fond of = fixed expression. Fond of music.

questions.question: By the time she graduates, she _______ studied for six years. (Future Perfect)
questions.explanation: Future Perfect: will have + V3. Will have studied.

questions.question: You _______ wear a seatbelt while driving. (obligation)
questions.explanation: Must = obligation. Must wear seatbelt.

questions.question: She arrived _______ the airport _______ time. (prepositions)
questions.explanation: Arrived at the airport (specific point). On time (time expression). At; on.

questions.question: _______ is your name? (WH-question word)
questions.explanation: Name question: What is your name?

questions.question: The boys were punished _______ misbehaving. (preposition after punished)
questions.explanation: Punished for = fixed preposition. Punished for misbehaving.

questions.question: She expressed her gratitude _______ the help she had received.
questions.explanation: Gratitude for = fixed expression. Grateful/gratitude for.

questions.question: Would you _______ closing the window? (polite request with 'would you mind')
questions.explanation: Would you mind + V-ing = polite request. Would you mind closing...

questions.question: _______ she was tired, she finished her work. (contrast)
questions.explanation: Although = contrast/concession. Although tired, she finished.

questions.question: He _______ a mistake in the report. (make - past)
questions.explanation: Past event: made a mistake. Simple past.

questions.question: She is the girl _______ father is a doctor. (possessive relative)
questions.explanation: Whose = possessive relative. The girl whose father is a doctor.

questions.question: He _______ his homework before he watched TV. (sequence of past events)
questions.explanation: Earlier past action: Past Perfect. Had completed.

questions.question: The news _______ shocking for everyone. (is/was/were - past)
questions.explanation: News = singular. Was shocking.

questions.question: She speaks English as _______ as her mother tongue. (comparison of equality)
questions.explanation: As...as for equal comparison with adverb: as well as.
